front: What is the formal definition of a sampling distribution?
back: The sampling distribution of a statistic is the probability distribution of that statistic, considered as a random variable, when derived from a random sample of size n. It describes the range of possible values the statistic can take and how likely they are.

front: Why is the Central Limit Theorem (CLT) crucial for the sampling distribution of the mean?
back: The CLT states that, for a large enough sample size (n ≥ 30), the sampling distribution of the sample mean will be approximately normally distributed, regardless of the shape of the population distribution. This allows for inference (e.g., confidence intervals, hypothesis tests) even when the population is not normal.

front: What is the standard error of a statistic and what does it measure?
back: The standard error is the standard deviation of a statistic's sampling distribution. It measures the variability or precision of the statistic as an estimator of a population parameter. For the mean, it is calculated as σ/√n.

front: How does the sample size (n) affect the sampling distribution?
back: As the sample size n increases: 1) The standard error decreases, making the statistic a more precise estimator. 2) The shape of the sampling distribution becomes more normal (as per the CLT). This is known as the Law of Large Numbers.

front: What is the key difference between the distribution of the sample data and the sampling distribution of a statistic?
back: The sample data distribution describes the variability of individual data points within a single sample. The sampling distribution describes the variability of a statistic (e.g., the mean) across many hypothetical samples of the same size from the population.
